summary: DHH reflects on how Basecamp repeated a mistake they've warned against for over a decade: promising a feature (custom domains for HEY) before shipping it. Despite knowing that roadmaps create an 'illusion of agreement' where customers project their own expectations onto vague promises, they still pre-announced the feature and faced inevitable disappointment from customers whose expectations didn't match reality. He argues that even meeting expectations produces only a muted 'finally!' rather than excitement. The post is a reminder to his future self that knowing something intellectually isn't enough — you have to live the lesson.
key_points: Roadmaps and feature promises create an 'illusion of agreement' where vague descriptions let everyone imagine different outcomes, guaranteeing disappointment for some
Even when you meet customer expectations from a pre-announced feature, the reaction is merely 'finally!' — not excitement or delight
Knowing something intellectually is insufficient; lessons must be lived and recalled in the moment to actually change behavior
The pressure of endless customer requests can push teams into making promises they know are counterproductive
money_quotes: It's not enough to know something, you have to live it. It's not enough to have learned something, you have to recall it.
When all we have to agree on is a slogan, like 'Meetings Tab, 4th Quarter', it becomes an empty imagination box that'll fit wildly different expectations.
Disappointment, however, is sure to ensue when only one set of expectations can actually be met.
But that just meant we met their expectations! Nothing too exciting, just, you know, finally!
key_insight: Pre-announcing features turns a potential moment of delight into guaranteed disappointment for some and mere relief for the rest, because vague promises let every customer imagine a different product.
